one_line_takeaway: A non-empirical, vendor-flavored position paper contending that off-the-shelf RAG is insufficient for compliance-regulated enterprises and proposing (but not executing) an evaluation framework for purpose-built enterprise RAG.
key_finding: The paper reports NO original quantitative results. The only percentage figures it contains—"5–10%" retrieval-quality gains and "3–8%" end-to-end accuracy increase—are explicitly hypothesized/aspirational and are attributed to prior, non-RAG-specific sources (Bughin et al. 2018, a McKinsey report; Brynjolfsson et al. 2023, an economics working paper), not measured by this work. Its substantive conclusion is qualitative: generic RAG cannot meet enterprise security, compliance, scalability, and integration demands, so purpose-built architectures plus rigorous (future) evaluation and industry "design partner" collaboration are required.
core_contribution: A single-author position/survey paper (Tilmann Bruckhaus, Strative) arguing that off-the-shelf, general-purpose Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is inadequate for enterprises—especially compliance-regulated industries like healthcare, finance, and legal—and that "purpose-built" enterprise RAG is needed. Despite the provocative title "RAG Does Not Work for Enterprises," the thesis is that generic RAG falls short and that enterprise deployments require (1) security/compliance (access controls, anonymization, auditability, HIPAA/GDPR/SOC2/CCPA), (2) accuracy and explainability, (3) scalability/performance, (4) seamless integration, and (5) customization/domain adaptation. Its named "contribution" is a PROPOSED evaluation framework (quantitative testing, qualitative analysis, ablation studies, industry case studies) for validating such solutions. It reviews cited techniques (dense vector indexes—HNSW, PQ, IVFADC—sparse encoders, hybrid queries) but does not introduce or evaluate a new method. It reads effectively as a vendor position paper for Strative's "RAG Enablement solution."
limitations: No empirical validation whatsoever: every experiment, dataset, baseline, and metric is proposed rather than performed, so none of the enterprise-RAG claims are substantiated by the paper's own evidence. The two quantitative figures are borrowed hypotheticals from unrelated (non-RAG, business/economics) sources. Single author affiliated with a commercial vendor (Strative), with recurring "RAG Enablement solution" and "design partners" framing, making it effectively a promotional position paper. Security/privacy/compliance are enumerated as requirements but not studied, so the paper offers no threat model, attack, defense, or privacy mechanism.

datasets_used: None actually used — the paper ran no experiments. NQ (Natural Questions), HotpotQA, TREC-COVID, and SQuAD are named only as datasets a future evaluation SHOULD use, plus unspecified 'proprietary enterprise datasets'.
quantitative_results: '5–10%' retrieval-quality gains — cited as a hypothesized/aspirational benefit from prior work (Bughin et al. 2018 McKinsey; Brynjolfsson et al. 2023), NOT measured by this paper.
'3–8%' end-to-end RAG accuracy increase from hybrid queries — stated conditionally ('could yield') and attributed to the same cited sources, NOT this paper's finding.
baselines_compared: None actually compared — the paper ran no experiments. DPR, ColBERT, and BM25 are named only as baselines a future head-to-head evaluation SHOULD run against.
